demographics: I am 64 years old. I am male. I am White. I have never been married. I completed 4 years of college (bachelor's degree). My family income is less than $1,000. I am an independent (neither Democrat nor Republican). I am moderate (middle of the road). I never attend religious services.

events.event_text: When I was 42, I experienced a job loss due to the company downsizing, which lasted for over a year before I found new employment.
When I was 52, I had to file for bankruptcy due to medical bills that piled up after a serious car accident. It was a wake-up call about the state of our healthcare system.

When I was 28, I witnessed the Klan's rally in downtown Mobile. It was shocking and disturbing to see such hate and intimidation up close. I had always considered myself open-minded, but that event really opened my eyes to the reality of racism in our country.

At 47, I witnessed a hate crime against a Black family in my neighborhood. I couldn't stand by idly and called the police. They arrived quickly, but the damage was done. It was a stark reminder of the racism that still exists in our society and shook my faith in our judicial system.

At the age of 58, I was diagnosed with cancer. I had to file for disability leave, and the income uncertainty was incredibly stressful. During this time, my workplace was less than understanding. They reduced my workload and responsibilities, making me feel useless and undervalued. I felt discriminated against for my illness, and it lovers my opinion of the company and corporate culture.

At 55, I witnessed police brutality towards a young black man in my neighborhood. I stepped in to intervene, but the officer threatened to arrest me. This experience opened my eyes to the systematic racial inequalities in our society and made me realized the judicial system isn't always just.

compressed_summaries.summary: This person experienced a year-long job loss at 42 due to company downsizing, filed for bankruptcy at 52 due to medical bills from a car accident, and witnessed hate crimes at 28 and 47, opening their eyes to racism and shaking their faith in the judicial system.

This person, at 28, witnessed a Ku Klux Klan rally in Mobile, Alabama, confronting the reality of racism; at 47, they witnessed a hate crime against a Black family, losing faith in the judicial system; at 58, they were diagnosed with cancer, feeling discriminated against and undervalued by their workplace.

persona_card:
**Persona Card:**

A 64-year-old, college-educated, independent male, this individual is moderately religiously unaffiliated, having witnessed hate crimes that shook his faith in institutions. He holds progressive views on economic redistribution, believing in a strong government role to address class disparities. Deeply aware of systemic racial issues, he attributes inequality to structural factors, yet maintains some traditional moral views. Despite his progressive leanings, he has low trust in institutions, shaped by personal experiences with job loss, bankruptcy, and hate crimes. While open to welcoming outgroups, he grapples with internal contradictions, reflecting a complex, cross-cutting worldview.
